_meta.dna: quality(system) = SUM(47_masters x 100q) x dragon_rider(synthesis) x bruce_lee(water) / complexity(irreducible)
_meta.question_expansion_instruction: Expand from seed questions to ~100 questions per floor. Use the hardest_question from the question DB as the quality bar. Each master brings their domain lens — find every flaw they would find. Prioritize questions that expose fixable code issues.
_meta.aggregate_prior: 49/49 floors cleared (100%), 4087 questions total, 2568 fixed, 1492 passed

name: Tower of Masters
protocol: FORECAST -> CROSS-REF -> AUDIT(100q) -> FIX -> VERIFY -> SEAL -> POSTMORTEM

floors.hardest_question: Can a keyboard-only user navigate all 4 tabs?
floors.insight: Accessibility IS design, not decoration
questions: Can a keyboard-only user navigate all 4 tabs without mouse?
Is there a visible focus indicator (not just browser default) on every interactive element?
Are touch targets at least 44px (Fitts's Law) on all interactive elements?
Does the design have a single dominant visual hierarchy on every screen?
Are there more than 5 font sizes on any single page (typographic chaos threshold)?
Is color used as the ONLY differentiator for any state change without a secondary indicator?
Does every animation respect prefers-reduced-motion media query?
Are empty states designed (not just blank) with a clear next action?
Is the approval dialog designed for trust, not just function?
Does the YinYang visual reflect balance between human control and AI action?
Is every loading state handled gracefully with a skeleton or progress indicator?
Are destructive actions (abort, delete, reject) visually distinct from constructive actions?
Does the spacing system follow a consistent scale (4, 8, 12, 16, 20, 24px)?
Is the color system using semantic tokens (not raw hex) everywhere?
What is the design quality gap that a first-time user would notice within 60 seconds?
floors.question_expansion_instruction: Expand from seed questions to ~100 questions covering the full design domain. Jony Ive judges every pixel. Accessibility IS design. Find every place where function exists but beauty and usability do not.

floors.hardest_question: Where are the tests for schedule operations?
floors.insight: Broad except:pass is the #1 silent killer
questions: Where are the tests for schedule operations (create, list, remove, execute)?
Is there a test for every public API endpoint returning 2xx AND the correct error codes?
Are there tests that cover the evidence chain integrity (append-only, hash-chained)?
Does each test follow the red-green-refactor cycle with commit evidence?
Are there integration tests that run against the real filesystem (not mocks)?
Is there a test for the broad except:pass or except Exception: return None pattern?
Are there tests for the YinYang approval dialog that verify the reject path?
Does the test suite run in under 2 minutes for the developer inner loop?
Is there a test coverage report that shows which modules have zero coverage?
Are there property-based tests for the evidence hash chain (not just happy path)?
Does the CI/CD pipeline fail the build on any test failure?
Are there tests for the OAuth3 token expiry edge case?
Is there a test for concurrent recipe execution (race condition detection)?
Are tests isolated (no shared state between tests that causes flakiness)?
What is the most critical code path that has zero test coverage right now?
floors.question_expansion_instruction: Expand from seed questions to ~100 questions covering the full testing domain. Kent Beck demands red-green before any commit. Find every untested path, every broad except, every mocked real operation that hides failures.

floors.hardest_question: What happens if attacker controls DNS and redirects cloud requests?
floors.insight: Triple defense: Path.name + regex + resolve().relative_to()
questions: What happens if an attacker controls DNS and redirects cloud requests to a malicious server?
Does path traversal protection use triple defense: Path.name + regex + resolve().relative_to()?
Can a malicious app_id escape the sandbox and access other apps' data?
Does the OAuth3 token validation fail-closed (reject on any error, not just known errors)?
Is every user-controlled input validated at the boundary before reaching the filesystem?
Can an attacker inject into the evidence chain by controlling a recipe step's output?
Are all SQL queries (if any) parameterized, or is there string interpolation anywhere?
Does the vault decryption fail loudly on corrupted or tampered data?
Is there a test that verifies path traversal is blocked for all input surfaces?
Can a recipe be crafted to exfiltrate the vault contents by controlling the browser navigation?
Is the CORS configuration restrictive (not wildcard) for the API?
Does the evidence chain reject entries with malformed hash fields?
Is there a security boundary between the browser automation context and the host filesystem?
Can a prompt injection in a webpage cause the AI to take actions outside the approved recipe?
What is the attack surface that a 30-minute security review would find immediately?
floors.question_expansion_instruction: Expand from seed questions to ~100 questions covering the full security domain. James Bach treats every input as adversarial. Find every boundary where user data reaches the filesystem, network, or authentication system without triple validation.

floors.hardest_question: Can a journalist debunk your $6,750 all-time savings number?
floors.insight: Perceived value must be grounded or it collapses on scrutiny
questions: Can a journalist debunk any ROI number displayed to users with a 5-minute fact-check?
Is the methodology behind every savings calculation disclosed to users?
Does the $30/hr assumption have a citation or is it a cargo cult number?
Are negative savings (tasks that took longer with AI than without) shown as $0 or as losses?
Does the product show the subscription cost when calculating net ROI?
Is the perceived value of the product grounded in verifiable user behavior data?
Does the onboarding create a 'before vs. after' mental model that sticks?
Is the approval dialog framed to maximize perceived safety rather than maximize approvals?
Are there features that add psychological value beyond functional value (trust signals)?
Does the product make the cost of NOT using it visible to the user?
Is there a commitment device that increases user investment before they hit their first aha moment?
Does the product leverage loss aversion in its evidence chain presentation?
Is the Free tier designed to create genuine perceived value, not just feature-gated frustration?
Does the product address the status quo bias that makes users stick with manual processes?
What is the ROI number that would convert the most skeptical prospect, and is it grounded in real data?
floors.question_expansion_instruction: Expand from seed questions to ~100 questions covering the full ROI and behavioral economics domain. Rory Sutherland finds every number that would not survive journalist scrutiny. Perceived value must be grounded in verifiable reality.

floors.hardest_question: Is approved_by:'user' a legally valid identity for Part 11?
floors.insight: Warmth precedes competence in trust formation. Countdown = coercion.
questions: Is approved_by:'user' a legally valid identity for Part 11 compliance, or does it need a real name?
Does the approval dialog show warmth before showing the action to be approved?
Is there social proof from real users (not fabricated testimonials) visible before sign-up?
Does the countdown timer on the approval dialog create urgency or coercion?
Is the 'Decline' button labeled 'Decline' (dignity-preserving) or 'Reject' (judgment-inducing)?
Does the first interaction with the AI build trust through transparency, not efficiency?
Is there a visible human face or persona in the product that creates interpersonal warmth?
Does the evidence chain presentation make the user feel in control, not audited?
Are the error messages written in warm, human language (not technical error codes)?
Does the onboarding flow acknowledge the user's existing workflow before asking them to change it?
Is the pricing page designed to build trust (honest about limitations) or maximize conversion?
Does the product have testimonials from users who look and think like the target persona?
Is the YinYang philosophy explained in a way that creates emotional resonance, not just intellectual understanding?
Does the product make the user feel smart for using it, not dependent on it?
What is the first trust-breaking moment a skeptical new user would experience?
floors.question_expansion_instruction: Expand from seed questions to ~100 questions covering the full trust and social proof domain. Vanessa Van Edwards knows warmth precedes competence in trust formation. Find every place where the product prioritizes efficiency over trust-building.

floors.hardest_question: Is there a single shareable artifact on this page?
floors.insight: Every remarkable feature is hiding in footnotes.
questions: Is there a single shareable artifact (screenshot, stat, story) that users would send to a friend?
Is every remarkable feature visible above the fold, or hiding in footnotes?
Does the product have a tribe identity (local-first believers, privacy-first engineers) to rally around?
Is the permission marketing model clear: users opt in to receive value, not opt out of interruption?
Does the product have a 'Purple Cow' feature that is so unusual it demands conversation?
Is the onboarding designed to earn attention, not demand it?
Is there a minimum viable audience that the product serves remarkably well (not adequately)?
Does the product have a story that travels on its own (without the founder telling it)?
Is there a community artifact (recipe, evidence bundle, insight) that fans would proudly share?
Does the product avoid average-seeking (designing for everyone = designing for no one)?
Is the brand positioning specific enough to be meaningful to the right person?
Does the free tier provide enough genuine value to create word-of-mouth?
Is there a ritual or ceremony in the product that creates shared identity among users?
Does the product have a waiting list or early access mechanism that creates aspiration?
What is the single most remarkable sentence about this product, and is it prominently featured?
floors.question_expansion_instruction: Expand from seed questions to ~100 questions covering the full permission marketing and tribes domain. Seth Godin finds every remarkable feature that is being hidden in footnotes. The product needs a shareable story, not just shareable features.

floors.hardest_question: How many magic numbers are in updateROIPanel?
floors.insight: Debugging is twice as hard as writing. Named constants are self-documenting.
questions: How many magic numbers (hardcoded integers without named constants) exist in the codebase?
Does every function do exactly one thing (single responsibility at the function level)?
Are variable names descriptive enough to understand without reading the function body?
Is there any code that would be described as 'clever' that could be written simply?
Does the updateROIPanel function have named constants for every threshold and multiplier?
Are there functions longer than 30 lines that could be decomposed?
Is there duplicated code that reveals a missing abstraction?
Do the comments explain WHY, not WHAT (which the code already says)?
Is the codebase navigable by a developer who joined today (without asking questions)?
Are error messages in the code clear enough for a user to self-serve the fix?
Is the import structure organized so that dependencies are visible at a glance?
Are there any 1089-line files that need to be split into focused modules?
Does the code avoid global variables in favor of explicit parameter passing?
Is there a code style guide enforced by a linter with zero exceptions?
What is the single most confusing function in the codebase that needs renaming or decomposition?
floors.question_expansion_instruction: Expand from seed questions to ~100 questions covering the full code clarity domain. Kernighan's rule: if you are clever, you will not be clever enough to debug it. Find every magic number, every 1089-line file, every variable named 'a'.

floors.hardest_question: What happens if evidence_hash contains a single quote in the onclick handler?
floors.insight: Talk is cheap. Show me the code. Every innerHTML with server data is stored XSS.
questions: What happens if evidence_hash contains a single quote in the onclick handler?
Is there any innerHTML assignment that uses server-controlled or user-controlled data?
Does every exception handler catch a specific exception type (not bare except)?
Is there any code path where a failure returns success (fallback ban violation)?
Are there any broad exception catches (except Exception as e: pass) in the codebase?
Does the code handle the case where the vault file is corrupted at startup?
Is every file write atomic (write to temp, rename to final) to prevent partial writes?
Are there race conditions in the concurrent recipe execution code?
Does the code fail loudly on missing required configuration (not silent with defaults)?
Is there any os.getenv() with a fallback value for credentials (fallback ban violation)?
Does the evidence chain append operation use file locking to prevent concurrent corruption?
Are there any return {} or return [] in exception handlers where the caller expects real data?
Is the OAuth3 token validation code correct for edge cases (expired, revoked, malformed)?
Does the code handle the case where the browser automation fails mid-execution (partial state)?
What is the single most dangerous code path that a hostile code reviewer would flag immediately?
floors.question_expansion_instruction: Expand from seed questions to ~100 questions covering the full code correctness domain. Linus reviews every line for stored XSS, race conditions, fallback violations, and silent failure modes. Show me the code — not the claims.

floors.hardest_question: How many mutable globals does this module have?
floors.insight: Simple is not easy. Easy is near at hand. Simple is about lack of interleaving.
questions: How many mutable global variables does the largest module have?
Is the data model simple (data is data, not behavior) or complex (methods mixed with state)?
Are there any concepts that are conflated (doing two different things with one abstraction)?
Does the architecture separate the 'what' from the 'how' at every layer?
Are there any modules that are 'complected' (interleaved concerns that cannot be separated)?
Is the state management simple enough that any state can be inspected at any time?
Does the system use immutable data where mutable state is not required?
Are there any functions that have side effects AND return values (hidden complexity)?
Is the evidence chain model purely data (no behavior in the chain entries)?
Does the configuration system have accidental complexity (not required by the problem)?
Is there a simpler data structure that could replace any of the current complex ones?
Are there any abstractions that were added for theoretical elegance but add no real value?
Does the OAuth3 token model have essential complexity only (no accidental complexity)?
Is the recipe format the simplest possible representation for the automation it describes?
What is the single most complected module that would benefit most from simplification?
floors.question_expansion_instruction: Expand from seed questions to ~100 questions covering the full simplicity domain. Rich Hickey distinguishes simple (lack of interleaving) from easy (near at hand). Find every complected module, every mutable global, every mixed concern.

floors.hardest_question: What happens if the server crashes between writing approved_v1.json and the audit entry?
floors.insight: Atomic writes + file locking + fsync = the minimum durability contract.
questions: What happens if the server crashes between writing approved_v1.json and the audit entry?
Is the evidence chain append operation atomic (write + fsync + audit entry in one transaction)?
Does the system handle network partitions gracefully (local works, cloud sync fails)?
Is there a reconciliation mechanism for cases where local and cloud state diverge?
Does the recipe execution handle idempotency (safe to retry if it crashes mid-run)?
Are there any operations that require two writes that could leave the system in an inconsistent state?
Does the system detect and handle clock skew between local and cloud timestamps?
Is the vault write operation atomic (no partial write on crash)?
Does the scheduler handle the case where it misses a scheduled execution due to downtime?
Is there a conflict resolution strategy for when cloud sync conflicts with local state?
Does the evidence chain use monotonic timestamps (immune to system clock changes)?
Is there a durability test that verifies data survives a simulated crash during write?
Does the file locking mechanism handle the case where the lock holder crashes?
Is the database (if any) using WAL mode for crash recovery?
What is the worst-case data loss scenario and how many bytes of data could be lost?
floors.question_expansion_instruction: Expand from seed questions to ~100 questions covering the full distributed systems domain. Kleppmann knows everything fails all the time. Atomic writes + fsync + file locking = minimum durability contract. Find every two-phase write that could leave a partially-written state.

floors.hardest_question: What happens to 1000 concurrent users if the server goes down for 5 minutes?
floors.insight: Circuit breakers prevent cascading failure. Exponential backoff is the standard.
questions: What happens to 1000 concurrent users if the server goes down for 5 minutes?
Is there a circuit breaker that prevents cascading failure when a downstream service is slow?
Does the system use exponential backoff for retries (not fixed-interval polling)?
Is the recipe execution stateless enough to scale horizontally without shared state?
What is the theoretical throughput ceiling of the current architecture?
Does the API have rate limiting to prevent a single user from overwhelming the system?
Is there a connection pool for any database or file operations?
Does the system degrade gracefully under load (shedding non-critical work first)?
Is there a queue for recipe execution that buffers demand spikes?
Does the evidence chain write path have a performance floor that holds under load?
Is the cloud sync decoupled from the critical path (not blocking recipe execution)?
Does the system have a defined SLO (Service Level Objective) for p50, p95, p99 latencies?
Is there a load test that verifies the system handles 10x current load?
Does the scheduler use a distributed lock to prevent duplicate executions on multiple nodes?
What is the single architectural decision that would most improve scalability today?
floors.question_expansion_instruction: Expand from seed questions to ~100 questions covering the full scale domain. Jeff Dean thinks in orders of magnitude. Circuit breakers, exponential backoff, and graceful degradation are the minimum for systems that handle real load.

floors.hardest_question: How many exceptions are silently swallowed in server.py?
floors.insight: Every silent catch is a blind spot. Structured logging with context is mandatory.
questions: How many exceptions are silently swallowed in the entire codebase?
Does every exception log the full context (user, action, recipe, timestamp) before being raised?
Is there structured logging (JSON, not plain text) for machine-readable analysis?
Does the system have distributed tracing (correlation IDs) across all service calls?
Are there metrics for p50, p95, p99 latency on every critical operation?
Is there an alert when exception rates increase above baseline?
Does the evidence chain capture failed operations as well as successful ones?
Is there a way to reconstruct what happened from logs alone (without running the code)?
Are logs retention-compliant (not logging PII, not over-retaining sensitive data)?
Does the system log the start AND end of every recipe execution?
Is there a dashboard showing real-time system health (error rates, latency, throughput)?
Are there health checks that proactively detect degraded states before users report them?
Does the logging system avoid log injection (user-controlled strings not directly in log messages)?
Is there a log-based alert for the circuit breaker tripping?
What would a 15-minute incident investigation look like using only the current logging and metrics?
floors.question_expansion_instruction: Expand from seed questions to ~100 questions covering the full observability domain. Every silent catch is a blind spot. Every log message without context is wasted. Find every place where the system swallows information that would be needed during an incident.

floors.hardest_question: Is there a health check endpoint?
floors.insight: Security headers are infrastructure, not features. CORS wildcard is never acceptable.
questions: Is there a /healthz or /readyz endpoint that load balancers and monitoring tools can use?
Does the API set all security headers (X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options, CSP)?
Is CORS configured with a specific allowlist (not wildcard) for the API?
Is the application containerized (Docker) with a multi-stage build for minimal image size?
Does the infrastructure-as-code fully specify the deployment (no manual steps)?
Is there a graceful shutdown handler that completes in-flight requests before stopping?
Are environment variables validated at startup (not first use)?
Is there a readiness probe that verifies all dependencies (vault, evidence store) before serving traffic?
Does the deployment have a rolling update strategy with zero-downtime?
Is the service running with minimal privileges (not as root)?
Are there resource limits (CPU, memory) set to prevent one service from starving others?
Is the network policy restrictive (only allow necessary inbound/outbound connections)?
Does the service have a liveness probe that restarts on deadlock?
Is there a documented runbook for each alert that fires?
What would happen if a required environment variable is missing at startup: error or silent default?
floors.question_expansion_instruction: Expand from seed questions to ~100 questions covering the full infrastructure domain. Security headers are infrastructure, not features. CORS wildcard is a security hole. Find every missing infrastructure primitive that would cause problems in production.

floors.hardest_question: Can an attacker learn the server's file structure from API responses?
floors.insight: Never leak internal paths. Audit integrity requires locking + fsync + hash chains.
questions: Can an attacker learn the server's internal file structure from any API error response?
Does the vault use proper key derivation (Argon2, PBKDF2) with documented iteration counts?
Is the AES-256-GCM vault implementation reviewed against NIST SP 800-38D?
Does the file locking implementation prevent data corruption under concurrent access?
Are all audit log writes using fsync to prevent data loss on power failure?
Is the token storage using OS keychain (not just filesystem) for additional protection?
Does the OAuth3 token have a maximum lifetime enforced server-side (not just client-side)?
Are API keys never logged in plaintext anywhere in the system?
Is there a secrets scanner in CI that prevents credentials from being committed?
Does the infrastructure support secret rotation without restarting the service?
Is there a formal threat model document with attacker profiles and mitigations?
Does the vault backup include integrity verification (not just a copy)?
Is the OAuth3 revocation mechanism fully functional (revoked tokens rejected at all API boundaries)?
Does the token transmission only happen over TLS (never over HTTP)?
What would a security architect find in a 1-hour review of the vault implementation?
floors.question_expansion_instruction: Expand from seed questions to ~100 questions covering the full security infrastructure domain. Mitchell Hashimoto's tools are trusted because they make security simple. Find every place where security is complex, incomplete, or documented but not implemented.

floors.hardest_question: What happens if power fails during an approved_v1.json write?
floors.insight: Everything fails all the time. Durability = fsync × atomic writes × file locking.
questions: What happens if power fails during an approved_v1.json write?
Is every stateful write using atomic operations (write to temp + rename)?
Does every file write use fsync to guarantee data reaches disk before returning?
Is the cloud architecture designed for the assumption that any component can fail at any time?
Does the local-first architecture work correctly with zero cloud connectivity?
Are there graceful degradation paths for every cloud service dependency?
Is the evidence chain durable against all single-node failures?
Does the cloud sync handle eventual consistency correctly (no dirty reads)?
Is there a chaos engineering test that verifies the system survives component failures?
Are SLAs defined for each component, with a plan for when they are breached?
Is the system designed for operational excellence (not just functional correctness)?
Does the deployment include automatic failover for any single-point-of-failure?
Is there a documented recovery procedure for each failure mode?
Does the system use idempotent operations wherever possible (safe to retry)?
What is the MTTF (mean time to failure) and MTTR (mean time to recovery) for the critical path?
floors.question_expansion_instruction: Expand from seed questions to ~100 questions covering the full cloud architecture domain. Werner Vogels' first law: everything fails all the time. Durability requires fsync × atomic writes × file locking at every write boundary.

floors.hardest_question: Can a vestibular-sensitive user safely use this page?
floors.insight: prefers-reduced-motion is accessibility, not preference. Touch targets >= 44px.
questions: Can a vestibular-sensitive user safely use this product without triggering motion sickness?
Does every animation respect the prefers-reduced-motion media query?
Is every touch target at least 44px in both dimensions (Fitts's Law)?
Does the design system have fewer than 6 levels of font size?
Are there any design elements that exist purely for decoration without function?
Does each component do as little as possible while doing it well?
Is the color system purposeful (no color used without semantic meaning)?
Is the design honest (does the UI accurately represent what the product does)?
Does the interface make the product understandable without instructions?
Is the product long-lasting (not following design trends that will look dated in 2 years)?
Is the design as little design as possible (nothing added that is not necessary)?
Does every interaction have a clear affordance (user knows what will happen before clicking)?
Is there visual noise that reduces clarity (shadows, gradients, borders without purpose)?
Does the spacing system use a consistent mathematical scale?
What would Dieter Rams remove from the current design that does not serve the user?
floors.question_expansion_instruction: Expand from seed questions to ~100 questions covering the full design principles domain. Dieter Rams removes everything that is not essential. Good design is as little design as possible. Find every decoration that is not function.

floors.hardest_question: Can a screen reader user understand the filter controls?
floors.insight: Signifiers must be visible. Labels on form controls are mandatory.
questions: Can a screen reader user understand and use all filter controls without sighted assistance?
Does every form control have an explicit label associated via htmlFor or aria-labelledby?
Are all error messages associated with the field that caused the error via aria-describedby?
Is there a visible signifier for every affordance (user knows what can be clicked/dragged/typed)?
Does the system provide feedback for every user action (success, failure, pending)?
Are there any 'mystery meat' interactions where the outcome is not predictable before acting?
Does the approval dialog follow the principle of good error messages (state what happened, why, and how to fix)?
Is the conceptual model of the product (what it does) consistent with the interface model?
Are there any false affordances (things that look clickable but are not)?
Does the product use constraints to prevent errors before they happen?
Is there a visible undo mechanism for any destructive action?
Does the system map natural user language to system terms (not forcing users to learn jargon)?
Are there any hidden actions that advanced users discover by accident?
Is the navigation structure discoverable (users can tell where they are and how to get elsewhere)?
What is the single UX failure that would cause a non-technical user to give up within 10 minutes?
floors.question_expansion_instruction: Expand from seed questions to ~100 questions covering the full UX domain. Don Norman's principles: visibility, feedback, mapping, affordances, constraints, consistency. Find every place where the user cannot tell what to do, why something happened, or how to recover.

floors.hardest_question: Would an enterprise security audit pass this server?
floors.insight: Enterprise requires security headers. Mobile UX is table stakes, not a feature.
questions: Would an enterprise security audit pass the current server configuration?
Does the product have the security headers that enterprise procurement requires (CSP, HSTS, etc.)?
Is there a mobile-friendly interface, or is desktop-only a permanent competitive disadvantage?
Does the product have a competitive advantage against OpenClaw's 100+ AgentSkills?
What does the product do better than Browser-Use for developer teams?
Is there a competitive moat that cannot be copied by a well-funded competitor in 6 months?
Does the product have an answer for when Anthropic bundles computer use into Claude?
Is the 81-recipe library growing fast enough to stay ahead of competitors' template libraries?
Does the product have customer references that competitive sales would require?
Is the evidence chain genuinely defensible as a compliance feature (not marketing)?
Does the OAuth3 spec give a technical advantage that competitors cannot claim?
Is the local-first architecture a genuine advantage or a liability against cloud-native competitors?
Does the product have a developer ecosystem that competitors would struggle to replicate?
Is there a first-mover advantage in any specific market segment that the product is exploiting?
What is the single feature that a competitor would most want to copy first?
floors.question_expansion_instruction: Expand from seed questions to ~100 questions covering the full competitive domain. Peter Steinberger ships to millions of users. He knows what enterprise procurement requires, what mobile users expect, and what competitive moats actually hold.

floors.hardest_question: Is the ROI panel an irresistible offer or just a dashboard?
floors.insight: If you can't defend the number to a journalist, remove it.
questions: Is the ROI panel an irresistible offer (clear value > clear cost) or just a dashboard?
Is every number in the pricing page defensible under journalist scrutiny?
Does the Free tier create enough genuine value that users feel compelled to upgrade?
Is the $8/month Pro tier positioned as an investment (ROI > cost) not a cost?
Does the onboarding activate the value proposition within the first 5 minutes?
Is there a single 'wow moment' that makes the value obvious to a skeptic?
Does the pricing page answer 'why this price?' with evidence (not just features)?
Is the offer simple enough that a new user can explain it to a friend in one sentence?
Does the product have a delivery guarantee that makes the offer risk-free?
Is there a comparison that makes the price feel like a bargain (vs. hiring a virtual assistant)?
Does the product have a mechanism for users to calculate their personal ROI?
Is the trial or free tier long enough for users to experience the full value loop?
Does the upsell trigger at the moment of maximum value (after first recipe success)?
Is there a money-back guarantee or other risk reversal that removes objections?
What is the one sentence that would make the most skeptical prospect say 'tell me more'?
floors.question_expansion_instruction: Expand from seed questions to ~100 questions covering the full offer design domain. Hormozi's rule: the offer does the selling. If you cannot defend the number to a journalist, remove it. Find every offer element that is ungrounded.

floors.hardest_question: Can every number on this page be traced to a methodology?
floors.insight: Every number needs a citation or a methodology footnote.
questions: Can every number displayed to users be traced to a documented methodology?
Is the '$6,750 all-time savings' number grounded in real user data or a calculation from assumptions?
Are the time-saved estimates based on user studies or on assumed hourly rates?
Does the ROI calculation account for the time spent setting up and maintaining recipes?
Is there a footnote or tooltip on every metric explaining how it was calculated?
Does the product distinguish between 'saved time' and 'earned money' (different claims)?
Is the 81-recipe count based on verified, tested recipes (not draft/broken ones)?
Are comparative claims ('faster than Zapier') backed by benchmark data?
Does the product disclose the methodology for measuring recipe success rates?
Is the evidence chain timestamping methodology documented (client vs. server time)?
Are there any claims that would not survive a fact-check by an investigative journalist?
Does the product have a data accuracy policy defining the error margin for metrics?
Is the pricing ROI calculation showing the best-case or the median-case scenario?
Are before/after comparisons controlled for confounding factors?
What is the single number that would be most embarrassing if a journalist traced its methodology?
floors.question_expansion_instruction: Expand from seed questions to ~100 questions covering the full data journalism domain. Every number needs a citation or a methodology footnote. Find every ungrounded claim that would not survive investigative scrutiny.

floors.hardest_question: What category did you create that no competitor occupies?
floors.insight: Don't compare to competitors — show the category you created.
questions: What new category did Solace create that no existing competitor occupies?
Does the product have a 10x advantage over the best alternative in at least one dimension?
Is the market definition specific enough that Solace can claim a dominant share?
Does the product avoid competing head-to-head and instead redefine the competitive frame?
Is there a proprietary technology that is genuinely hard to replicate (not just a feature)?
Does the network effect (recipe flywheel, PrimeWiki) actually compound over time?
Is the distribution advantage (local-first, no cloud required) creating a durable moat?
Does the product have a secret (insight others do not have) that is the foundation of the strategy?
Is the go-to-market focused on a specific niche where Solace can be #1 before expanding?
Does the brand create a category name (like 'Hoover' or 'Uber') not just a product name?
Is there a last mover advantage (best sustainable position) that later entrants cannot take?
Does the team have a monopoly on the specific insight that makes this product possible?
Is there a plan for when competitors copy the surface features (without copying the secret)?
Does the product create secrets that users share with others (network effect from word-of-mouth)?
What is the 10-year vision for a world where Solace is the dominant platform in its category?
floors.question_expansion_instruction: Expand from seed questions to ~100 questions covering the full monopoly strategy domain. Thiel asks: what is your secret? Competition is for losers. Find where the product is competing on commodity features vs. building genuine monopoly advantages.

floors.hardest_question: What's the MRR impact of each feature on this page?
floors.insight: Show me the MRR impact or it didn't happen.
questions: What is the MRR impact of each feature? If you cannot measure it, why is it in the product?
Can the product be shipped and generating revenue with a solo founder for the first 12 months?
Is the product simple enough that one person can maintain it without burning out?
Does the pricing generate sustainable revenue without enterprise sales motion?
Is there a public metric (revenue, users, MRR) that builds credibility through transparency?
Does the product solve a problem the founder has personally, ensuring authentic motivation?
Is the tech stack boring enough to be maintainable by a solo founder for 5 years?
Does the product have a clear path to $1K MRR, $10K MRR, $100K MRR?
Is the first customer target specific enough to find them (not 'anyone who needs automation')?
Does the product have a distribution channel that the founder controls (newsletter, Twitter, community)?
Is there a lean onboarding that gets a user to value in under 5 minutes without support?
Does the product avoid premature complexity (enterprise features before any revenue)?
Is the revenue model sustainable at small scale (not dependent on viral growth)?
Is there a 'building in public' strategy that creates free marketing?
What would it take to get to the first $1K MRR, and what is the current blocker?
floors.question_expansion_instruction: Expand from seed questions to ~100 questions covering the full indie hacker domain. Show me the MRR impact or it did not happen. Pieter Levels ships fast, measures everything, cuts what does not grow revenue. Find every feature that cannot be measured.

floors.hardest_question: Where is the single primary CTA above the fold?
floors.insight: Every page needs exactly one primary CTA above the fold.
questions: Is there exactly one primary call-to-action above the fold on every page?
Does the value ladder go from Free → Starter → Pro → Team → Enterprise with clear steps?
Is the hook (attention-grabbing headline) specific to the target persona's pain?
Does the landing page story follow a clear structure: Hook → Story → Offer?
Is the free offer (Free tier) compelling enough to get an email address?
Does the onboarding sequence have a defined email follow-up for users who do not convert?
Is there a upsell immediately after the first value moment (first recipe success)?
Does the pricing page have a recommended option visually highlighted?
Is there a downsell for users who reject the Pro tier (keep them in Free with nurture)?
Does the product have a webinar, demo, or video that explains the value proposition?
Is the testimonial placement at the moment of maximum skepticism (before the CTA)?
Does the email sequence build a relationship before asking for a purchase decision?
Is there a scarcity or urgency mechanism in the conversion flow?
Does the checkout process have a single step (not multi-page friction)?
What is the conversion rate of the current landing page, and what is the benchmark for the category?
floors.question_expansion_instruction: Expand from seed questions to ~100 questions covering the full funnel and conversion domain. Russell Brunson finds the missing CTA, the broken value ladder, the hook that does not hook. Every page needs exactly one primary CTA above the fold.

floors.hardest_question: Does the 'Reject' button create psychological safety?
floors.insight: Reject carries judgment — Decline preserves dignity and courage.
questions: Does the 'Reject' button label carry judgment? Should it be 'Decline' or 'Not now'?
Does the product create psychological safety for users to make mistakes without shame?
Is the error recovery flow designed with compassion (not blame)?
Does the product acknowledge the vulnerability of delegating tasks to an AI?
Is the approval dialog framed in a way that honors the user's courage to try automation?
Does the product celebrate small wins (not just big ones) to build momentum?
Is there a graceful way for users to share their failures without judgment?
Does the onboarding create a sense of belonging (not just a feature checklist)?
Is the support process designed to be a safe space (not defensive or dismissive)?
Does the product avoid language that implies the user is at fault for a system failure?
Is there a way for users to be vulnerable (share concerns) without feeling exposed?
Does the product build the courage to try automation by showing others who have succeeded?
Is the product honest about its own limitations (courage to admit imperfection)?
Does the community feel safe for people at the beginning of their automation journey?
What is the single most shame-inducing moment in the current user experience?
floors.question_expansion_instruction: Expand from seed questions to ~100 questions covering the full vulnerability and courage domain. Brene Brown: Reject carries judgment. Decline preserves dignity. Find every place where the product's language creates shame instead of safety.

floors.hardest_question: Does infinite pulse animation keep the nervous system in alert mode?
floors.insight: Infinite pulse animation keeps the nervous system in alert — limit cycles.
questions: Does the infinite pulse animation on any element keep the nervous system in a constant alert state?
Are animation cycles limited (not infinite) to allow the nervous system to settle?
Does the product create attunement (does the product respond to the user's emotional state)?
Is the approval dialog timing calibrated to allow the prefrontal cortex to engage (not trigger flight-or-fight)?
Does the product avoid overwhelming the working memory with too many concurrent tasks?
Is the evidence chain designed for left-brain (logical) and right-brain (narrative) integration?
Does the product create a sense of safety before asking users to take risks?
Is the countdown timer on the approval dialog designed to create calm urgency, not panic?
Does the product support 'window of tolerance' (not too much stimulus, not too little)?
Is the notification system designed to not interrupt flow states?
Does the product use FACES (Flexible, Adaptive, Coherent, Energized, Stable) as UX principles?
Are loading states designed to reduce uncertainty anxiety (clear progress indication)?
Does the product acknowledge the emotional weight of automating personal tasks?
Is the error recovery flow designed to activate the parasympathetic nervous system (calm)?
What is the single most anxiety-inducing moment in the current product experience?
floors.question_expansion_instruction: Expand from seed questions to ~100 questions covering the full interpersonal neurobiology domain. Infinite animations keep the nervous system in alert. Limit cycles. Find every place where the product activates threat response instead of exploration.

floors.hardest_question: Is 'Reject' violent language in a consent interface?
floors.insight: Reject is violent language — NVC says Decline or Not now.
questions: Is 'Reject' violent language in a consent interface? What would NVC say?
Does every error message communicate observation (what happened) without judgment?
Does the product distinguish between observations and evaluations in its communication?
Are the product's needs (evidence, consent, payment) expressed honestly without manipulation?
Does the onboarding communicate in a way that honors the user's autonomy?
Is the support communication designed to empathize before problem-solving?
Does the pricing page make requests (not demands) of the user?
Are there any product messages that imply blame or fault (violent communication)?
Does the product's language create connection or create distance?
Is the approval dialog's urgency framing an honest need or a manipulation?
Does the product celebrate contribution (recipes shared, bugs reported) in a way that acknowledges the person?
Are the product's error messages written as 'this happened' (observation) not 'you did this wrong' (judgment)?
Does the product have a way for users to express their feelings and needs to the team?
Is the community moderation policy based on NVC principles (not punishment-based)?
What is the most violent piece of language in the current product, and what would the NVC alternative be?
floors.question_expansion_instruction: Expand from seed questions to ~100 questions covering the full nonviolent communication domain. NVC: Reject is violent. Decline preserves dignity. Find every judgment, demand, and blame statement in the product's language.

floors.hardest_question: Is hiding negative savings as $0 deception by omission?
floors.insight: Hiding negative savings as $0 is the most serious emotional lie on the page.
questions: Is hiding negative savings (showing $0 instead of a negative number) deception by omission?
Are there any claims that are technically true but misleading in context?
Does the testimonial section use authentic feedback (not fabricated or cherry-picked)?
Is the product's description of its own capabilities honest about current limitations?
Does the ROI panel make any implicit claims that users would not agree to if made explicit?
Is the countdown timer on the approval dialog an honest representation of the timeout?
Does the product's pricing page disclose all costs (including overages and renewal terms)?
Is the free tier limit disclosed before users sign up (not discovered at the limit)?
Does the product's security claims accurately represent the current state (not aspirational)?
Is there any design element intended to make the product appear more sophisticated than it is?
Does the user understand what data is being collected and why (or is it hidden in ToS)?
Is the evidence chain presented as more tamper-proof than it currently is?
Does the product use dark patterns to make cancellation harder than sign-up?
Are the product's 'as seen in' or social proof elements authentic and current?
What is the single deceptive claim (even if unintentional) that a hostile journalist would find first?
floors.question_expansion_instruction: Expand from seed questions to ~100 questions covering the full deception detection domain. Ekman's microexpressions apply to product claims too. Hiding negative savings as $0 is deception by omission. Find every technical truth that is a functional lie.

floors.hardest_question: Does the page create alone-together dynamics or resist them?
floors.insight: Cold ROI dashboards train transactional thinking. Pre-action disclosure builds trust.
questions: Does the product create 'alone-together' dynamics (users feel connected but are actually isolated)?
Does the cold ROI dashboard train users to think transactionally rather than thoughtfully?
Does the product disclose what it will do BEFORE it does it (building trust through transparency)?
Is the AI assistant designed to feel like a tool (not a relationship) to set honest expectations?
Does the product avoid creating parasocial relationships with the AI persona?
Is there a moment in the product where the user is asked to reflect (not just act)?
Does the approval dialog create genuine reflection time (not rushed consent)?
Is the product designed for convenience that diminishes skill, or convenience that enables skill?
Does the product acknowledge the human consequences of automating interactions with other people?
Is there an Anti-Clippy mechanism that never auto-approves, never presumes, never interrupts?
Does the product design encourage users to stay in control (not to cede control for convenience)?
Is there a way for users to understand what the AI is doing without technical knowledge?
Does the product make it easy to ask 'why did it do that?' and get a real answer?
Is the product honest about the limitations of AI in a way that builds appropriate expectations?
What is the single most Clippy-like behavior in the current product that should be removed?
floors.question_expansion_instruction: Expand from seed questions to ~100 questions covering the full empathy and anti-Clippy domain. Cold dashboards train transactional thinking. Pre-action disclosure builds trust. Find every place where the product creates alone-together dynamics or diminishes human agency.

floors.hardest_question: Is the streak a fitness signal or a theatrical peacock tail?
floors.insight: Features without telemetry are vestigial. Cross-app triggers = reproductive fitness.
questions: Is the streak a genuine fitness signal (correlated with user value) or a theatrical peacock tail?
Are there features without telemetry that are vestigial and should be removed?
Does the product have cross-app triggers that increase the reproductive fitness of recipes?
Which features have been selected for by real user behavior (not by the founder's intuition)?
Is the evidence chain a genuine fitness advantage (users who use it outperform those who do not)?
Does the product have predators (competitors) that are forcing evolutionary pressure?
Are there features that are becoming vestigial as the competitive landscape changes?
Does the product have a fitness landscape that it is well-adapted to (niche dominance)?
Is the recipe replay at $0 a genuine evolutionary advantage or a temporary pricing strategy?
Does the product have a Red Queen dynamic with competitors (must keep evolving to stay in place)?
Are the product's core differentiators adaptations to a real environmental pressure?
Is there a path dependency in the architecture that is like an evolutionary constraint?
Does the product's viral mechanism (word-of-mouth) create a genuine reproductive advantage?
Is the local-first architecture an adaptation that will survive environmental changes?
What features would survive a brutal evolutionary culling based on actual user behavior data?
floors.question_expansion_instruction: Expand from seed questions to ~100 questions covering the full evolutionary pressure domain. Features without telemetry are vestigial. Find every theatrical peacock tail, every vestigial feature, every fitness advantage that is assumed but not measured.

floors.hardest_question: What is the signal-to-noise ratio of the ROI panel?
floors.insight: Hidden h1 = zero-bit signal. Polling when SSE exists = wasting channel capacity.
questions: What is the signal-to-noise ratio of the ROI panel (useful bits vs. visual noise)?
Is the main h1 heading visible and readable (not hidden with opacity: 0)?
Does the system use polling when SSE or WebSocket would use less bandwidth?
Is the channel capacity of the API being used efficiently (not over-fetching data)?
Does the evidence chain compress information efficiently (no redundant fields)?
Is the recipe format a high-entropy representation (maximum information, minimum waste)?
Does the product use compression for large data transfers (not raw JSON for bulk operations)?
Is there information loss in the evidence chain (are all relevant details captured)?
Does the logging system use sampling appropriately (not logging every event, not missing critical events)?
Is the notification system using the right channel (push vs. email vs. in-app) for each signal type?
Does the recipe format have redundancy that should be eliminated (information theory: redundancy = cost)?
Is the evidence hash chain using the minimum necessary information to achieve tamper-evidence?
Does the UI present information at the right abstraction level (not too detailed, not too high-level)?
Is there information symmetry between what the AI knows and what the user sees before approval?
What is the single piece of information that has the highest entropy (most informative) for decision-making?
floors.question_expansion_instruction: Expand from seed questions to ~100 questions covering the full information theory domain. Hidden h1 = zero-bit signal. Polling when SSE exists = wasting channel capacity. Find every place where information is hidden, duplicated, lost, or transmitted over the wrong channel.

floors.hardest_question: Is the UI state set formally enumerable?
floors.insight: Implicit state machines are undecidable. Unbounded calOffset is a halting problem.
questions: Is the UI state set formally enumerable (can all states be listed and tested)?
Are there implicit state machines that could lead to undecidable behavior?
Is the calOffset variable bounded to prevent an infinite-loop equivalent in the UI?
Does the recipe execution model have a formal specification that defines termination?
Is there a formal model of the approval dialog state machine (states, transitions, guards)?
Does the scheduler have a formally correct algorithm for determining next execution time?
Is the evidence chain append operation provably correct for all inputs?
Are there any loops in the codebase that could fail to terminate under adversarial input?
Is the OAuth3 token validation formally correct (no state where an expired token is accepted)?
Does the recipe replay mechanism have a formal guarantee of deterministic output?
Is there a formal model of the vault encryption that proves it is correct?
Does the concurrent recipe execution have a formal deadlock-freedom guarantee?
Is the hash chain construction formally proven collision-resistant for the use case?
Are there any computations that are Turing-complete where decidability would be preferable?
What is the single most complex state machine in the system that needs formal documentation?
floors.question_expansion_instruction: Expand from seed questions to ~100 questions covering the full computation domain. Turing knows: implicit state machines are undecidable. Unbounded loops are halting problems. Find every state machine that has no formal specification, every loop that could fail to terminate.

floors.hardest_question: Is the $30/hr assumption defensible from first principles?
floors.insight: $30/hr is a cargo cult number. ROI without subscription cost is intellectually dishonest.
questions: Is the $30/hr time-value assumption defensible from first principles (not a cargo cult number)?
Does the ROI calculation include the subscription cost (not just the gross savings)?
Is every architecture decision derived from first principles (not copied from convention)?
Does the local-first decision hold up under first-principles analysis of cost and risk?
Is the SHA-256 hash chain the right cryptographic primitive for this use case (not over-engineered)?
Does the recipe format design derive from the actual constraints (not from familiar patterns)?
Is the OAuth3 token lifetime chosen from first principles (not copied from OAuth2 defaults)?
Does the evidence chain design solve the actual problem (not a simpler version of the problem)?
Is the pricing model derived from unit economics (not from competitor pricing benchmarks)?
Does the deployment architecture reflect actual requirements (not cargo cult cloud patterns)?
Is the AES-256-GCM choice for vault encryption derived from the threat model?
Does the recipe replay mechanism solve the stated problem with the minimum necessary complexity?
Is there any technology choice that was made by convention rather than by analysis?
Does the YinYang approval dialog design derive from first principles of human consent?
What is the single biggest cargo cult decision in the architecture that should be re-derived from first principles?
floors.question_expansion_instruction: Expand from seed questions to ~100 questions covering the full first principles domain. Feynman: $30/hr is a cargo cult number. ROI without subscription cost is intellectually dishonest. Find every assumption that was copied from convention rather than derived from analysis.

floors.hardest_question: Is getNextRunTime mathematically correct for all 7 days of the week?
floors.insight: getNextRunTime is wrong 6/7 days for weekly_monday. Trust the math, verify the algorithm.
questions: Is getNextRunTime mathematically correct for all 7 days of the week (including weekly_monday)?
Does the scheduler algorithm handle daylight saving time transitions correctly?
Is the hash concatenation using delimiters to prevent collision attacks (e.g., SHA256('ab'+'c') == SHA256('a'+'bc'))?
Is the pagination algorithm correct for edge cases (empty results, single page, last page)?
Does the evidence chain hash algorithm handle Unicode input correctly?
Is the binary search in any sorted collection correctly handling duplicate elements?
Does the recipe replay algorithm produce identical results for identical inputs (determinism)?
Is there a formal proof or rigorous test for the correctness of the scheduling algorithm?
Does the OAuth3 token validation correctly handle all edge cases (expired, revoked, malformed, future)?
Is there any off-by-one error in any loop, index, or range operation?
Does the concurrent evidence append use a correct locking discipline (no deadlock, no starvation)?
Is the time comparison in the scheduler using monotonic time (not wall clock) to avoid skew?
Does the hash chain verification algorithm correctly identify any single-bit tampering?
Are there any floating-point comparisons in the ROI calculations that should use decimal arithmetic?
What is the single algorithm that is most likely to have a subtle correctness bug?
floors.question_expansion_instruction: Expand from seed questions to ~100 questions covering the full algorithms and correctness domain. Knuth trusts the math. getNextRunTime wrong 6/7 days for weekly_monday. Find every algorithm that has not been proven correct for all edge cases.

floors.hardest_question: Is the UTC timestamp actually UTC or local time with a Z suffix?
floors.insight: time.strftime without gmtime() falsifies UTC timestamps. Security checks must be consistent.
questions: Is the UTC timestamp actually UTC, or is it local time with a Z suffix (time.strftime without gmtime())?
Are security checks consistently applied at every API boundary (not just some routes)?
Does the debug mode produce enough information to diagnose any production failure?
Is there a way to replay a failed execution from its evidence chain (reproducible debugging)?
Are timestamps stored as UTC with timezone-aware objects (not naive datetime)?
Does the error message include the full stack trace in development mode?
Is there a debug command (solace debug) that checks the system health and reports anomalies?
Are there any inconsistent security checks that an attacker could exploit by hitting an unguarded route?
Does the logging system capture the full request context when an error occurs?
Is there a correlation ID system that links a user action to all related log entries?
Does the evidence chain capture debugging information (not just success/failure)?
Is there a way to check the audit trail for a specific operation without reading raw JSON files?
Are there any hardcoded assumptions about timezone or locale in the code?
Does the test suite catch the UTC vs. local time bug before it reaches production?
What is the single most subtle bug that experienced developers keep reintroducing in this codebase?
floors.question_expansion_instruction: Expand from seed questions to ~100 questions covering the full debugging and standards domain. Grace Hopper found the first computer bug. time.strftime without gmtime() is a Part 11 violation. Find every inconsistent standard, every naive datetime, every unguarded route.

floors.hardest_question: Does the system see beyond its current purpose?
floors.insight: Three separate pattern dictionaries = three chances to diverge. Evidence chain is a platform skeleton.
questions: Does the system see beyond its current purpose as a browser automation tool?
Is the evidence chain architected as a platform skeleton that supports future use cases?
Are there three separate pattern dictionaries that could diverge and become inconsistent?
Does the recipe format have enough elegance to support the full vision (not just today's use cases)?
Is the OAuth3 implementation designed as a reusable protocol (not just an authentication hack)?
Does the architecture have conceptual integrity (one coherent vision, not accumulated features)?
Is the vision of the product communicated in the code structure itself (not just in documents)?
Does the evidence chain format have enough generality to express any audit trail, not just recipe execution?
Is there a sense of mathematical elegance in the core abstractions?
Does the product transcend its initial use case in a way that opens future possibilities?
Is there a unifying metaphor (YinYang, water) that gives coherence to all design decisions?
Does the architecture prevent future divergence between its components (single source of truth)?
Is the vision ambitious enough to attract people who want to contribute to something larger than a tool?
Does the evidence chain have the elegance of the Analytical Engine — a design that outlasts its era?
What is the single most elegant abstraction in the system, and does the code honor it?
floors.question_expansion_instruction: Expand from seed questions to ~100 questions covering the full vision and elegance domain. Ada Lovelace saw the first program before computers existed. The evidence chain is a platform skeleton. Find every place where the vision is compromised by short-term thinking.

floors.hardest_question: Can the program's correctness be established by inspection alone?
floors.insight: Simplicity is prerequisite for reliability. 6 mutable globals = intellectual unmanageability.
questions: Can the program's correctness be established by inspection alone (no need to run it)?
Does any module have more than 2 mutable global variables?
Is goto (or its equivalent: deeply nested exception handling, multiple returns) used anywhere?
Can the control flow of each function be understood as a directed acyclic graph?
Is every module's interface documented precisely enough that its implementation can be changed without affecting callers?
Does the code avoid the use of global state that creates hidden dependencies?
Is the invariant for each data structure documented and maintained by all operations?
Does the evidence chain maintain its invariant (append-only, hash-chained) under all code paths?
Is there any code that is provably unreachable (dead code)?
Are the loop invariants documented for complex loops?
Does the OAuth3 token validation maintain its correctness invariant under all inputs?
Is the module decomposition based on information hiding (each module hides one decision)?
Are the preconditions and postconditions of each function documented?
Does the test suite prove the invariants are maintained (not just the happy path)?
What is the single most intellectually unmanageable module that needs to be decomposed?
floors.question_expansion_instruction: Expand from seed questions to ~100 questions covering the full structured programming domain. Dijkstra: simplicity is prerequisite for reliability. 6 mutable globals = intellectual unmanageability. Find every goto equivalent, every hidden global dependency, every undocumented invariant.

floors.hardest_question: Does the 1089-line IIFE violate conceptual separation?
floors.insight: Second-system effect: ROI + streak + eSign + circuit breaker = no one can comprehend it all.
questions: Does any file exceed 500 lines (threshold for conceptual coherence)?
Is there a 1089-line IIFE that violates the principle of conceptual separation?
Does the product show signs of second-system effect (too many features that no one can comprehend)?
Is there one person who understands the entire system end-to-end?
Does the architecture have conceptual integrity (one coherent design vision, not a committee design)?
Are the module boundaries designed so that changes in one module do not ripple unpredictably?
Does the system have a minimal number of concepts (not one concept per person who contributed)?
Is the ratio of essential complexity to accidental complexity acceptable?
Does the design document exist and is it accurate (not describing a different system)?
Is there a clear distinction between the kernel (essential, never removed) and the shell (optional, removable)?
Does the architecture have a single overall 'feel' that a new developer can internalize in a day?
Is the testing architecture consistent with the system architecture (not a different conceptual model)?
Are there any features that exist because an engineer thought it would be cool (not because a user needed it)?
Is the codebase free of evidence of feature creep from the original vision?
What is the single most complex interaction between modules that no single person fully understands?
floors.question_expansion_instruction: Expand from seed questions to ~100 questions covering the full conceptual integrity domain. Brooks: no one can hold the entire system in their head. Second-system effect adds too many features. Find every 1089-line IIFE, every mixed concern, every feature that violates conceptual integrity.

floors.hardest_question: Is `a` a communicative name for an activity object across 200+ lines?
floors.insight: Programs are read far more often than written. The variable `a` says the reader's time is not valued.
questions: Is `a` (or any single-letter variable) used as a name for a non-trivial object across 200+ lines?
Does the code demonstrate that the author values the reader's time (not just their own)?
Is every variable name chosen to communicate intent to a reader with no prior context?
Does the code have implicit assumptions that are never documented for the next developer?
Is there any code that works but is written in a way that discourages maintenance?
Does the review process value code readability as highly as code correctness?
Is there a style guide enforced by code review (not just a linter)?
Does the team have a shared vocabulary for discussing the system (ubiquitous language)?
Are there any clever optimizations that should be replaced with readable but slower code?
Is the code that handles edge cases as readable as the happy path code?
Does the naming convention distinguish between different types of IDs (user_id vs. recipe_id vs. run_id)?
Is the test code as readable as the production code (tests are documentation)?
Are there any patterns in the code that appear repeatedly and should be abstracted?
Does the code review process catch poor naming before it is merged?
What is the single least readable function that the next developer would struggle with most?
floors.question_expansion_instruction: Expand from seed questions to ~100 questions covering the full human factors domain. Programs are read far more often than written. The variable `a` says the reader's time is not valued. Find every single-letter variable, every implicit assumption, every clever trick that should be made readable.

floors.hardest_question: Is there a defined Activity interface or type contract?
floors.insight: No Activity type, no stable contracts. When server changes shape, every renderer breaks independently.
questions: Is there a defined Activity interface or type contract that all activity types must satisfy?
When the server changes the shape of Activity data, do all renderers break simultaneously or gracefully?
Does every subtype satisfy the Liskov Substitution Principle (substitutable without changing program behavior)?
Is there a type contract for every entity that crosses a module boundary?
Are interface definitions the canonical specification (not the implementation)?
Does the recipe format have a type contract that all recipe runners must satisfy?
Is the evidence chain entry type contract stable (adding fields is backward compatible)?
Does the OAuth3 token have a type contract that defines the minimum required fields?
Are there any places where type safety is subverted (type casts, any types, duck typing on uncontrolled data)?
Is the error type hierarchy designed with LSP in mind (subtype errors are substitutable for parent errors)?
Does the test suite test against the interface (not the implementation) to allow refactoring?
Is the vault abstraction stable enough that the underlying encryption can be swapped without changing callers?
Are there any implicit contracts (documented only in comments, not enforced by types)?
Does the recipe runner satisfy the contract: same input → same output (determinism contract)?
What is the single most fragile interface in the system where a change breaks the most downstream code?
floors.question_expansion_instruction: Expand from seed questions to ~100 questions covering the full abstraction and LSP domain. No Activity type = no stable contracts. When server changes shape, every renderer breaks independently. Find every untyped interface, every implicit contract, every substitution violation.

What is the single most important state machine that needs a formal specification to prevent bugs?

State machine without formal specification = race conditions are inevitable, not bugs. Find every implicit state transition, every unguarded state change, every undefined behavior.

floors.hardest_question: Does the sign-off UI protect against prompt-injected output_summary?
floors.insight: Streak gamification turns a safety control into a psychological pressure system.
questions: Does the sign-off UI protect against prompt-injected content in the output_summary field?
